items.q: What determines whether a star becomes a white dwarf or a neutron star?
items.correct: Its mass - low/medium mass becomes a white dwarf; massive stars produce neutron stars or black holes.
items.misconception: Its colour during its main sequence phase.
items.why: Students think colour determines end state. Mass is the key factor - low-mass stars (like the Sun) end as white dwarfs; high-mass stars supernova, leaving neutron stars or black holes depending on mass.

items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: Why are Type Ia supernovae used as 'standard candles'?
items.correct: They have nearly uniform peak brightness, so their apparent brightness reveals distance.
items.misconception: Because they happen at regular time intervals.
items.random_wrong: Because they are the brightest objects in the universe.
items.why: Students confuse standard candle with regular timing. The key property is CONSISTENT INTRINSIC BRIGHTNESS - if you know the actual luminosity, the observed dimness tells you the distance.
The answer examiners want is: Carbon. “Hydrogen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A star with hydrogen shell burning around an inert helium core is a:
items.why: It's Red giant. “Main-sequence star” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate temperature in millions of Kelvin needed to start helium burning?
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
